meta.title: E343 Full Course Alignment Graph
meta.description: Indiana-centric course alignment graph mapping Indiana Academic Standards, Van de Walle, Illustrative Mathematics, Five Practices (Smith & Stein), CDM Talk Moves, Canvas assignments, and E343 lesson plans.
meta.scope: Full course — Units 1-4 (Pedagogy Foundations, Discourse, Operations & Algebraic Thinking, Fractions)

sources.indiana_standards: Indiana Academic Standards for Mathematics (via Learning Commons Connector / CASE Network) — PRIMARY
sources.ccss_m: Common Core State Standards — folded as metadata into Indiana standards where equivalences exist
sources.van_de_walle: Elementary and Middle School Mathematics: Teaching Developmentally (Van de Walle et al.)
sources.cdm: Classroom Discussions in Math / Talk Moves, 3rd Edition (Chapin, O'Connor, Anderson)

topics.label: Addition/Subtraction Problem Structures
topics.description: Four problem types: join (add to), separate (take from), part-part-whole (put together/take apart), compare. Varying the unknown position changes the operation needed.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle uses 'join/separate/part-part-whole/compare'; CCSS uses 'add to/take from/put together-take apart/compare'; Indiana mirrors CCSS language.

topics.label: Basic Fact Fluency
topics.description: Developing accuracy, automaticity, and flexibility with single-digit addition/subtraction facts. Strategies include counting on, making ten, decomposing, using doubles, and related facts.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle distinguishes three phases: counting strategies → reasoning strategies → mastery. Indiana uses 'demonstrate fluency.' CCSS says 'fluently add and subtract.'

topics.label: Number Talks
topics.description: Structured classroom routine where students mentally solve problems and share strategies. Develops number sense and computational fluency through discourse.
topics.terminology_note: Not a standards term. Comes from Parrish (2010). Van de Walle discusses similar ideas under 'classroom discourse.' Five Practices framework structures the teacher moves within number talks.

topics.label: Multiplicative Problem Structures
topics.description: Five types: equal groups, multiplicative comparison, array/area, combination/Cartesian product. Each yields different meanings for multiplication.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 9 names all five. Indiana 3.CA.3 lists 'equal-sized groups, arrays, area models, equal intervals on a number line.' CCSS 3.OA.A.1 focuses on 'groups of objects.'

topics.label: Two Meanings of Division
topics.description: Sharing (partition): know number of groups, find group size. Measurement (quotative): know group size, find number of groups. Same notation, different conceptual structure.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 9 uses 'partition/measurement.' Indiana 3.CA.4 uses 'partitioning, sharing, and inverse of multiplication.' CCSS 3.OA.A.2 describes both without naming them.

topics.label: Multiplication Fact Strategies
topics.description: Strategic sequence for learning multiplication facts: start with 2s, 5s, 10s (skip counting foundation), then use doubling, near-squares, and derived fact strategies for harder facts like 6×7, 7×8, 8×9.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 10 organizes by strategy families. Indiana 3.CA.6 says 'demonstrate fluency with mastery.' CCSS 3.OA.C.7 says 'know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.'

topics.label: Multi-Digit Multiplication Strategies
topics.description: Four strategies: complete number, partitioning (partial products), compensation, and clustering. Area model and base-ten block representations connect place value to the standard algorithm.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 10 names the four strategies. Indiana 4.CA.1 says 'strategies based on place value and properties of operations.' CCSS 4.NBT.B.5 adds 'rectangular arrays and/or area models.'

topics.label: Division Algorithm and Strategies
topics.description: Long division as notation for sharing/measurement thinking. Base-ten blocks make the algorithm concrete: distribute tens first, regroup, then distribute ones. Remainders add interpretive complexity.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 13 treats the algorithm as the last step in a progression from concrete to abstract. Indiana 4.CA.2 says 'strategies based on place value.' CCSS 4.NBT.B.6 says 'equations, rectangular arrays, and/or area models.'

topics.label: Vertical Articulation K-2
topics.description: How number sense, computation, and algebraic thinking standards build across K, 1st, and 2nd grade. Understanding the progression helps teachers set appropriate expectations and connect to prior knowledge.
topics.terminology_note: Not a standards term. Used in curriculum planning to describe how a strand develops across grade levels.

topics.label: Mathematical Process Standards
topics.description: Problem solving, reasoning and proof, communication, connections, representation. Indiana process standards parallel CCSS Standards for Mathematical Practice (SMP).
topics.terminology_note: Indiana uses 'Process Standards' (PS.1-PS.8). CCSS uses 'Standards for Mathematical Practice' (MP.1-MP.8). Van de Walle integrates process throughout. Five Practices framework operationalizes discourse-based process standards.

topics.label: Five Practices Framework Introduction
topics.description: The Five Practices (anticipating, monitoring, selecting, sequencing, connecting) as a framework for orchestrating productive mathematical discourse. Shifts teacher role from questioner-of-one to orchestrator-of-many.
topics.terminology_note: Smith & Stein's framework. Unit 1 introduces the structure; Unit 2 deepens each practice via CDM (Chapin, O'Connor, Anderson).

topics.label: Launch-Explore-Summarize Lesson Structure
topics.description: Three-phase lesson design: Launch (introduce task, activate prior knowledge, do NOT provide solution method), Explore (students work individually/pairs/groups while teacher monitors), Summarize (whole-class discussion using Five Practices). IM embeds this as Warm Up → Activities → Synthesis → Cool Down.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle uses 'before/during/after.' IM uses 'Launch-Work-Synthesize.' CDM uses 'Launch-Explore-Summarize.' Same structure, different names.

topics.label: Learning Goals vs. Performance Goals
topics.description: Learning goals specify what students should UNDERSTAND (conceptual). Performance goals specify what students should be able to DO (observable). A student can achieve a performance goal without the learning goal (e.g., executing an algorithm without understanding place value).
topics.terminology_note: Five Practices Ch. 2 emphasizes this distinction. IM 'lesson goals' are learning goals. Indiana standards blend both but lean procedural.

topics.label: Talk Moves
topics.description: Strategic questioning techniques for productive mathematical discourse. Four steps: (1) clarify/share own thoughts (wait time, turn & talk, revoicing, say more), (2) orient to others' thinking (who can repeat?), (3) deepen reasoning (press for reasoning, agree/disagree), (4) deepen and engage (who can add on?).
topics.terminology_note: From CDM (Chapin, O'Connor, Anderson). The Five Practices framework assumes these moves are in place. Van de Walle discusses similar ideas as 'classroom discourse.'

topics.label: Assessing vs. Advancing Questions
topics.description: Assessing questions surface current student thinking without leading or correcting ('Tell me what you did,' 'How did you get that?'). Advancing questions push thinking forward toward the lesson goal ('What if the numbers were different?', 'Can you show me with a picture?'). Both types are prepared during anticipating.
topics.terminology_note: From Five Practices Ch. 3-4. CDM calls similar moves 'probing questions.' Van de Walle's 'during' phase uses the same distinction informally.

topics.label: Think Aloud Interviews (TAI)
topics.description: One-on-one formative assessment where the teacher listens to a student verbalize thinking while solving a problem. Teacher does NOT teach, correct, or lead. Reveals understanding vs. mere procedural performance.
topics.terminology_note: Course-specific assessment tool. Not a standards term. Activities sourced from Van de Walle or IM.

topics.label: Unit Fractions and Fraction Concepts
topics.description: A unit fraction 1/b is the quantity formed by one part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts. All fractions a/b are built by iterating 1/b. The referent whole must be identified for any fraction to be meaningful. Partitioning (dividing into equal parts) and iterating (repeating a unit fraction) are the two foundational actions.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 15 uses 'part-whole,' 'measurement,' 'division,' and 'operator' constructs. Indiana 3.NS.2 says 'iterations of unit fractions.' CCSS 3.NF.A.1 uses identical language.

topics.label: Equivalent Fractions
topics.description: Two fractions are equivalent when they represent the same quantity. Different partitionings of the same whole yield different fraction names for the same amount. Visual models (area, length, set) show that the number and size of parts change but the quantity stays the same.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 15 emphasizes visual models before the multiply-top-and-bottom rule. Indiana 3.NS.4/4.NS.3 say 'fraction models.' CCSS 4.NF.A.1 says 'visual fraction models.'

topics.label: Fraction Comparison Strategies
topics.description: Four strategies: same denominator (compare numerators), same numerator (compare denominators — larger denominator means smaller pieces), benchmarks (compare each fraction to 0, 1/2, or 1), closeness to benchmarks (e.g., both near 1 but which is closer?). Comparisons are valid only when fractions refer to the same whole.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 15 details all four. Indiana 4.NS.4 names 'common denominators or numerators' and 'benchmark such as 0, 1/2, and 1.' CCSS 4.NF.A.2 uses identical language.

topics.label: Division as the Source of Fractions
topics.description: a ÷ b = a/b. Division with remainders connects to mixed numbers (e.g., 7 ÷ 4 = 1 remainder 3 = 1 3/4). Fair-share cutting makes this concrete: 3 loaves shared among 5 people means each gets 3/5 of a loaf.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 15 treats this as a fraction 'construct' (division construct). Indiana 5.NS.2 says 'division of whole numbers by whole numbers.' CCSS 5.NF.B.3 uses identical framing.

topics.label: Fractions to Decimals Connection
topics.description: Tenths and hundredths connect fractions to decimal notation via powers of 10. Base-ten blocks can represent fractional amounts (flat = 1, rod = 1/10, unit = 1/100). Mental equivalents: 1/2 = 0.5, 1/4 = 0.25, 3/4 = 0.75.
topics.terminology_note: Indiana 4.NS.5 says 'tenths and hundredths in decimal and fraction notations.' CCSS 4.NF.C.5 says 'denominator 10 as equivalent fraction with denominator 100.'

topics.label: Fraction Multiplication (Introductory Context)
topics.description: Multiplication of fractions begins with 'fraction of a fraction' contexts (e.g., 1/2 of a 3/4 pizza = 3/8). Area models make the multiplicative structure visible. This extends whole-number multiplication to the fractional domain.
topics.terminology_note: Van de Walle Ch. 16. Indiana standards place fraction multiplication at grade 5. CCSS 5.NF.B.4.

topics.label: Classroom Discourse in Mathematics
topics.description: The practice of structuring mathematical talk so that students generate knowledge through articulation, access others' thinking, develop mathematical language and vocabulary, build reasoning habits, and create a learning community where ideas are valued.
topics.terminology_note: CDM (Chapin, O'Connor, Anderson) is the primary source for Unit 2. Van de Walle integrates discourse throughout. Five Practices operationalizes discourse. IM's synthesis phase is a discourse structure.

topics.label: Equity in Mathematical Participation
topics.description: Deliberately expanding who gets positioned as a mathematical thinker. Considerations: who has recently presented, representation of diverse students, pre-alerting shy students, building confidence through partner work before whole-class sharing. Selection and sequencing decisions have equity implications.
topics.terminology_note: Emphasized in Five Practices Ch. 5. CDM discusses equitable participation. Not a standards term but central to the course pedagogy.

indiana_standards.description: Count to at least 100 by ones and tens. Count by one from any given number.
Write whole numbers from 0 to 20 and identify number words from 0 to 10.
Say the number names in standard order when counting objects, pairing each object with one and only one number name.
Identify sets of 1 to 10 objects in patterned arrangements and tell how many without counting (subitizing).
Identify whether the number of objects in one group is greater than, less than, or equal to the number of objects in another group.
Solve real-world problems that involve addition and subtraction within 10 using modeling with objects or drawings.
Use objects or drawings to model the decomposition of numbers less than 10 into pairs in more than one way.
Find the number that makes 10 when added to the given number for any number from 1 to 9.
Create, extend, and give an appropriate rule for simple repeating and growing patterns with numbers and shapes.
Count to at least 120 by ones, fives, and tens from any given number.

Demonstrate fluency with addition facts and the corresponding subtraction facts within 20. Use strategies such as counting on, making ten, decomposing a number leading to a 10, using the relationship between addition and subtraction, and creating equivalent but easier or known sums.

ccss_equivalents.description: Add and subtract within 20, demonstrating fluency for addition and subtraction within 10. Use strategies such as counting on; making ten; decomposing a number leading to a ten; using the relationship between addition and subtraction; and creating equivalent but easier or known sums.

indiana_standards.description: Solve real-world problems involving addition and subtraction within 20 in situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all parts.

ccss_equivalents.description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions.

indiana_standards.description: Using number sense and place value strategies, add within 100, including adding a two-digit number and a one-digit number, and adding a two-digit number and a multiple of 10.
Count by ones, twos, fives, tens, and hundreds up to at least 1,000 from any given number.

Solve real-world problems involving addition and subtraction within 100 in situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all parts. Use estimation to decide whether answers are reasonable.

ccss_equivalents.description: Use addition and subtraction within 100 to solve one- and two-step word problems involving situations of adding to, taking from, putting together, taking apart, and comparing, with unknowns in all positions.
Use addition to find the total number of objects arranged in rectangular arrays with up to 5 rows and up to 5 columns; write an equation to express the total as a sum of equal addends.

indiana_standards.description: Using number sense and place value strategies, add and subtract within 1,000, including composing and decomposing tens and hundreds.
Show that the order in which two numbers are added (commutative property) and how the numbers are grouped in addition (associative property) will not change the sum.
Read and write whole numbers up to 10,000. Use words, models, standard form, and expanded form.
Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using strategies and algorithms based on place value, properties of operations, and relationships between addition and subtraction.
Solve real-world problems involving addition and subtraction of multi-digit whole numbers.
Model the concept of multiplication of whole numbers using equal-sized groups, arrays, area models, and equal intervals on a number line. Model the properties of 0 and 1 in multiplication.

ccss_equivalents.description: Interpret products of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 5 × 7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each.

indiana_standards.description: Model the concept of division of whole numbers with the following models: partitioning, sharing, and an inverse of multiplication. Model the properties of 0 and 1 in division.

ccss_equivalents.description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares.

indiana_standards.description: Multiply and divide within 100 using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division or properties of operations.

ccss_equivalents.description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.

indiana_standards.description: Demonstrate fluency with mastery of multiplication facts and corresponding division facts of 0 to 10.

ccss_equivalents.description: Fluently multiply and divide within 100. By the end of Grade 3, know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers.

indiana_standards.description: Solve real-world problems involving whole number multiplication and division within 100 in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities.

ccss_equivalents.description: Use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems in situations involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities.

indiana_standards.description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations.

ccss_equivalents.description: Multiply a whole number of up to four digits by a one-digit whole number, and multiply two two-digit numbers, using strategies based on place value and the properties of operations.

indiana_standards.description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division.

ccss_equivalents.description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division.

indiana_standards.description: Show how the order in which two numbers are multiplied (commutative property) and how numbers are grouped in multiplication (associative property) will not change the product. Investigate and apply the distributive property.

ccss_equivalents.description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to multiply and divide.

indiana_standards.description: Investigate the mathematical relationship between factors and multiples for whole numbers from 1-100.

Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and two-digit divisors using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division.

ccss_equivalents.description: Find whole-number quotients and remainders with up to four-digit dividends and one-digit divisors, using strategies based on place value, the properties of operations, and/or the relationship between multiplication and division.

indiana_standards.description: Solve real-world problems involving multiplication and division of whole numbers. In division problems that involve a remainder, explain how the remainder affects the solution.

Model unit fractions as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into equal parts; model non-unit fractions as the quantity formed by iterations of unit fractions. [Denominators: 2, 3, 4, 6, 8.]

ccss_equivalents.description: Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b.

indiana_standards.description: Model a non-unit fraction on a number line by marking equal lengths from 0, identifying each part as a unit fraction and locating the non-unit fraction as the endpoint.
Use fraction models to represent two simple equivalent fractions with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ even though the quantities are the same (e.g., 1/2 = 2/4, 4/6 = 2/3).

ccss_equivalents.description: Explain equivalence of fractions in special cases, and compare fractions by reasoning about their size.

indiana_standards.description: Compare two fractions with the same numerator or the same denominator by reasoning about their size based on the same whole.

ccss_equivalents.description: Explain equivalence of fractions in special cases, and compare fractions by reasoning about their size.

indiana_standards.description: Model mixed numbers and improper fractions using visual fraction models such as number lines and area models.

Use fraction models to represent two equivalent fractions with attention to how the number and size of the parts differ. Use this principle to generate equivalent fractions. [Denominators: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 25, 100.]

ccss_equivalents.description: Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n×a)/(n×b) by using visual fraction models.
Understand a fraction a/b as a multiple of 1/b.

indiana_standards.description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators (e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark such as 0, 1/2, and 1). Comparisons valid only when fractions refer to the same whole.

ccss_equivalents.description: Compare two fractions with different numerators and different denominators, e.g., by creating common denominators or numerators, or by comparing to a benchmark fraction such as 1/2.

indiana_standards.description: Write tenths and hundredths in decimal and fraction notations. Use words, models, standard form, and expanded form to represent decimal numbers to hundredths. Mentally calculate fraction and decimal equivalents for halves and fourths.

ccss_equivalents.description: Express a fraction with denominator 10 as an equivalent fraction with denominator 100.

indiana_standards.description: Compare two decimals to hundredths by reasoning about their size based on the same whole.
Use a number line to compare and order fractions, mixed numbers, and decimals to thousandths.
Explain different interpretations of fractions, including as parts of a whole, parts of a set, and division of whole numbers by whole numbers.

ccss_equivalents.description: Interpret a fraction as division of the numerator by the denominator (a/b = a ÷ b).

indiana_standards.description: Add and subtract fractions and mixed numbers with unlike denominators using strategies or the standard algorithm.

Solve real-world problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions referring to the same whole, including cases of unlike denominators. Use benchmark fractions and number sense to estimate and assess reasonableness.
Determine whether a group of up to 20 objects has an odd or even number of members.

ccss_equivalents.description: Determine whether a group of objects (up to 20) has an odd or even number of members.

indiana_standards.description: Solve real-world problems involving multiplication of whole numbers, including multiplicative comparison (e.g., by using drawings and equations with a variable for the unknown number to represent the problem).

ccss_equivalents.description: Interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison, e.g., interpret 35 = 5 × 7 as a statement that 35 is 5 times as many as 7.

indiana_standards.description: Use visual fraction models to multiply a fraction by a fraction or a whole number.

ccss_equivalents.description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
Interpret multiplication as scaling (resizing).
Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers.

indiana_standards.description: Use visual fraction models and numbers to divide a fraction by a fraction or a whole number.

ccss_equivalents.description: Apply and extend previous understandings of division to divide unit fractions by whole numbers and whole numbers by unit fractions.

learning_components.description: Interpret the product of two whole numbers as the number of groups times the size of each group, or vice versa
Identify a multiplication situation based on a given multiplication expression
Model a multiplication situation, including by writing a multiplication expression
Fluently divide within 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division or properties of operations
Fluently multiply within 100, using strategies such as the relationship between multiplication and division or properties of operations
Know from memory all products of two one-digit numbers
Multiply a four-digit number by a one-digit number using strategies based on place value or properties of operations
Multiply a three-digit number by a one-digit number using strategies based on place value or properties of operations
Multiply a two-digit number by a two-digit number using strategies based on place value or properties of operations
Use models to explain the multiplication of two two-digit numbers
Find whole-number quotients (no remainders) of two-digit dividends and one-digit divisors
Find whole-number quotients (with remainders) two-digit dividends and one-digit divisors
Find whole-number quotients (no remainders) of three-digit dividends and one-digit divisors
Use models to explain the division of whole numbers (with and without remainders) with two-digit dividends and one-digit divisors

vdw_sections.title: Developing Meanings for the Operations
vdw_sections.description: Covers the four additive problem structures (join, separate, part-part-whole, compare), five multiplicative structures (equal groups, comparison, array, area, combination), and two meanings of division (partition and measurement). Emphasizes contextual understanding before symbolic work.
Detailed treatment of join, separate, part-part-whole, and compare problem types. Shows how varying the unknown position changes cognitive demand.
Five multiplicative problem types and the partition/measurement distinction for division. Includes the convention that 4 × 8 means four sets of eight.

vdw_sections.title: Developing Basic Fact Fluency
vdw_sections.description: Three phases of fact development: counting strategies → reasoning strategies → mastery. Covers addition/subtraction fact strategies (making ten, doubles, near-doubles) and multiplication fact strategies (doubling, skip counting, derived facts).

vdw_sections.section: Addition and Subtraction Fact Strategies (approx. pp. 165-175)
vdw_sections.description: Specific strategies for developing addition/subtraction fact fluency: counting on, making ten, decomposing to ten, doubles, near-doubles.
Strategic sequence for multiplication facts: 2s, 5s, 10s first (skip counting); then derived facts using doubling, near-squares, and break-apart strategies for 6×7, 7×8, 8×9.

vdw_sections.title: Strategies for Whole-Number Computation (Multiplication and Division)
vdw_sections.description: Four multiplication strategies (complete number, partitioning, compensation, clustering). Area model as bridge to standard algorithm. Division algorithm as notation for concrete distribution. Base-ten block modeling throughout.

vdw_sections.title: Classroom Discourse and Teaching Through Problem Solving
vdw_sections.description: Van de Walle's pedagogical framework emphasizes teaching through problem solving: posing worthwhile tasks, letting students struggle productively, and orchestrating discussion. This aligns directly with the Five Practices framework. Van de Walle's 'before/during/after' lesson structure maps onto anticipating/monitoring/connecting.

vdw_sections.title: Developing Fraction Concepts
vdw_sections.description: Four fraction constructs: part-whole, measurement, division, operator. Emphasis on unit fractions as building blocks, visual models (area, length, set), and understanding equivalence through different partitionings rather than multiply-top-and-bottom rules.

vdw_sections.title: Developing Strategies for Fraction Computation
vdw_sections.description: Operations with fractions built on conceptual understanding. Fraction multiplication via area models. Division of fractions via measurement and missing-factor interpretations. Connection between fractions and decimals via base-ten representations.

vdw_sections.title: Teaching Through Problem Solving: Goals, Tasks, and Assessment
vdw_sections.description: Van de Walle's pedagogical framework for task selection, goal setting, and formative assessment. Before/During/After lesson structure. Think-aloud interviews as formative assessment tools. Guided reading notes (before/about/after your lesson content).

im_units.title: Adding and Subtracting Story Problems
im_units.description: Students solve and represent story problems involving addition and subtraction. Emphasis on making sense of problem structures and relating addition to subtraction.

im_units.title: Adding within 100
im_units.description: Students develop strategies for adding within 100 using place value understanding. Connects to number talks and derived-fact strategies.

im_units.title: Addition and Subtraction on the Number Line
im_units.description: Number line as model for addition/subtraction. Builds toward fluency with addition/subtraction within 100.

im_units.title: Equal Groups
im_units.description: Foundational work with equal groups using arrays and repeated addition. Bridge from additive to multiplicative thinking.

im_units.title: Introducing Multiplication
im_units.description: Students learn multiplication as equal groups and arrays. Division introduced as finding unknown group size or number of groups.

im_units.title: Area and Multiplication
im_units.description: Connects multiplication to area. Students tile rectangles and relate the area to multiplication expressions.

im_units.title: Relating Multiplication to Division
im_units.description: Students relate multiplication and division through equal groups, arrays, and area. Develop fluency with multiplication facts.

im_units.title: Multiplicative Comparison
im_units.description: Students interpret multiplication as comparison (e.g., 3 times as many). Extends multiplicative thinking beyond equal groups.

im_units.title: Multiplying and Dividing Multi-Digit Numbers
im_units.description: Students multiply up to 4-digit by 1-digit and 2-digit by 2-digit. Division with remainders using place-value strategies. Area model connects to standard algorithm.

im_units.title: From Multiplication to Division
im_units.description: Multi-digit division with two-digit divisors. Students use partial quotients and connect to the standard algorithm.

im_units.title: Fractions as Numbers
im_units.description: Students develop understanding of fractions as numbers. Unit fractions, non-unit fractions on number lines, comparing fractions with same numerator or denominator.

im_units.title: Extending Operations to Fractions
im_units.description: Students build on fraction concepts to generate equivalent fractions, compare fractions using benchmarks and common denominators, and begin adding/subtracting fractions.

im_units.title: Fraction Equivalence and Comparison
im_units.description: Deep work on fraction equivalence through visual models, comparison strategies using benchmarks and common denominators.

im_units.title: Decimals
im_units.description: Students connect fractions with denominators 10 and 100 to decimal notation. Compare decimals using visual models and place value reasoning.

im_units.title: Multiplying and Dividing Fractions
im_units.description: Students multiply fractions and whole numbers, explore division of fractions, and interpret fractions as division.

five_practices.title: Introduction to the Five Practices
key_ideas: The Five Practices framework structures productive mathematical discourse
Discussion is not show-and-tell; it must advance specific mathematical goals
The framework shifts teacher role from questioner-of-one to orchestrator-of-many

five_practices.title: Setting Goals and Selecting Tasks
key_ideas: Mathematical goals should specify what students will understand, not just do
Task cognitive demand determines the ceiling for student thinking
Goals and tasks together define what is possible in a lesson

five_practices.title: Anticipating Student Responses
key_ideas: Solve the task yourself multiple ways before teaching
Predict student strategies, representations, and misconceptions
Prepare assessing questions (surface thinking) and advancing questions (push further)

five_practices.title: Monitoring Student Work
key_ideas: Monitoring builds a real-time map of student strategies in the room
Assessing questions make student thinking visible; advancing questions push it further
Teacher tracks which anticipated strategies appeared and which did not
Post-lesson reflection: Did I interact with each group? What was unexpected?

five_practices.title: Selecting and Sequencing Student Solutions
key_ideas: Selecting determines what mathematical ideas students get to grapple with
Sequencing builds a coherent mathematical storyline (concrete → abstract)
Equity matters: who gets positioned as a mathematical thinker
Errors and partial solutions can be sequenced purposefully
Five challenges: relevance, expanding presenters, majority stuck, missing strategy, sequencing errors
example_tasks: Mr. Strong's Hike (Grade 5, division by fractions)

five_practices.title: Connecting
key_ideas: Connecting is where mathematical learning consolidates
Teachers press for comparison between strategies, not just sequential sharing
Generalizations must be made explicit, not left implicit
Connections tie student-generated strategies back to the lesson's mathematical goal

five_practices.label: Setting Goals and Selecting Tasks
five_practices.description: Before instruction: establish clear, specific mathematical goals and select tasks whose cognitive demand matches those goals. Goals should specify what students will understand, not just what they will do. Task selection determines the ceiling of possible student thinking.
teacher_moves: Identify learning goals tied to specific mathematical ideas
Select tasks with high cognitive demand
Anticipate how task features connect to goals

five_practices.label: Anticipating
five_practices.description: Before instruction: predict the range of strategies students might use, including correct approaches, partial understandings, and misconceptions. Anticipating prepares the teacher to recognize and respond to student thinking in real time rather than being surprised by it.
teacher_moves: Solve the task multiple ways yourself
Predict likely student strategies and representations
Identify potential misconceptions and partial understandings
Prepare assessing and advancing questions for each anticipated strategy

five_practices.label: Monitoring
five_practices.description: During instruction: circulate while students work to observe and understand their mathematical thinking. Monitoring is not checking for right answers — it is building a real-time map of what strategies are present in the room and what mathematical ideas they surface.
teacher_moves: Circulate to every group
Use assessing questions to surface student thinking
Use advancing questions to push students further
Track which strategies appear and who produced them
Note which anticipated strategies are absent

five_practices.label: Selecting and Sequencing
five_practices.description: During/after student work: choose which solutions to share in whole-class discussion and in what order. Selection determines what mathematical ideas the class has the opportunity to grapple with. Sequencing builds a coherent mathematical storyline — typically from concrete to abstract, from common to sophisticated, or from partial to complete.
teacher_moves: Select solutions that advance the mathematical goal
Consider equity: who gets positioned as a mathematical thinker
Sequence from concrete to abstract
Decide where to place errors or partial solutions
Pre-notify selected students

five_practices.label: Connecting
five_practices.description: During whole-class discussion: help students see relationships between different solution strategies and connect those strategies to the mathematical goal. Connecting is where the mathematical learning happens — it is not enough to share strategies; the teacher must orchestrate explicit comparison, contrast, and generalization across solutions.
teacher_moves: Ask students to compare strategies
Highlight mathematical relationships between solutions
Connect student work to the lesson's mathematical goal
Press for generalizations that go beyond specific numbers
Make explicit what might otherwise remain implicit

process_standards.description: Make sense of problems and persevere in solving them.
Construct viable arguments and critique the reasoning of others.
Look for and make use of structure.
Look for and express regularity in repeated reasoning.

cdm_chapters.source: Classroom Discussions in Mathematics (Chapin, O'Connor, Anderson)
cdm_chapters.title: Classroom Discussions in Mathematics (CDM)
key_ideas: Talk is critical: generates knowledge, gives access to others' thinking, develops vocabulary and reasoning
Four steps of talk moves: clarify/share → orient to others → deepen reasoning → engage
Specific moves: wait time, turn & talk, revoicing, say more, who can repeat, press for reasoning, agree/disagree, who can add on

cdm_videos.title: Wait Time: Hands down; let him think
cdm_videos.description: In this clip, Mrs. Foley reinforces respectful behaviors in the midst of a problem-solving lesson

cdm_videos.title: Turn-and-Talk: How many cans of grape juice?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Foley's students are solving a complicated problem with two kinds of packs of juice cans

cdm_videos.title: Turn-and-Talk: How do we know those are greater than a whole?
cdm_videos.description: Jaehun gives his thinking about where 7/6 and 13/9 are on a number line, a point of confusion

cdm_videos.title: Turn-and-Talk: Can everyone turn and repeat that idea?
cdm_videos.description: Ms. Fournier asks everyone to practice saying an important idea about ratios

cdm_videos.title: Revoicing: So you're saying I can't buy one can...?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Foley asks a student what 'at least twenty-six cans' means, revoicing to clarify

cdm_videos.title: Revoicing: So you multiplied the length...?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Foley's class is working on ways to find the volume of a rectangular prism

cdm_videos.title: Say More: Can you tell me what you did?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Luizzi's students have just done a turn-and-talk. She asks one student to report

cdm_videos.title: Say More: Tell me a little bit more
cdm_videos.description: Mr. Danella asks a student to give an argument as to why his claim is wrong

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Repeat? He's not all done yet
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Hayward's students are working on circling groups of hearts on a card with seven hearts

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Repeat? She was counting by fives
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Delaney's class is using a hundreds chart to explore counting patterns

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Repeat? Finding the volume of a rectangular prism
cdm_videos.description: A student gives his way of finding volume. Mrs. Foley asks others to repeat

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Repeat? How did you know that seven-eighths was greater than three-fourths?
cdm_videos.description: A student explains reasoning about fractions on a number line. Mrs. Rowan asks others to repeat

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Repeat? Which table is 'the root beer drinkers?'
cdm_videos.description: Ms. Fournier asks another student to repeat a classmate's solution strategy to a ratio problem

cdm_videos.title: Press for Reasoning: Are you sure? How do you know?
cdm_videos.description: Ms. Powers asks a student to explain her thinking about a strategy for subtracting numbers

cdm_videos.title: Press for Reasoning: Can you prove it?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Burgess's students are solving a mystery number puzzle. She presses for reasoning

cdm_videos.title: Press for Reasoning: Why does that work?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Foley presses for reasoning in students' explanations of volume computation

cdm_videos.title: Do You Agree or Disagree... and Why? Why do you agree with him?
talk_moves_demonstrated: Do You Agree or Disagree... and Why?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Delaney asks students whether they agree or disagree with what was said

cdm_videos.title: Do You Agree or Disagree... and Why? Do almost all of the people do their homework before dinner?
talk_moves_demonstrated: Do You Agree or Disagree... and Why?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Schineller asks students to turn and talk about whether they agree with a data claim

cdm_videos.title: Do You Agree or Disagree... and Why? Not to be rude but...
talk_moves_demonstrated: Do You Agree or Disagree... and Why?
cdm_videos.description: Mr. Danella asks students whether they agree with Jeda's claim about solving a puzzle

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Add On? Would you like to add on to the beginning of her thinking?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Delaney asks for input from others when a student reaches an impasse

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Add On? Who can add on to that?
cdm_videos.description: Ms. Powers asks a student to add on to a classmate's explanation about measurement

cdm_videos.title: Who Can Add On? What is a rectangular prism?
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Foley asks her students to weigh in with their understandings of what a rectangular prism is

cdm_videos.title: Setting Up Classroom Talk Norms: Discussing Fair Turns and Repeating
cdm_videos.description: Mrs. Hayward works with her kindergarten/Grade 1 students to review what constitutes a 'fair turn'

cdm_videos.title: Setting Up Classroom Talk Norms: How do we agree and disagree?
talk_moves_demonstrated: Do You Agree or Disagree... and Why?
cdm_videos.description: Ms. Luipold discusses with her students how to use the agree/disagree move appropriately

terminology_crosswalk.description: Maps equivalent terms across resources to reduce cognitive overload when instructors coordinate between Van de Walle, Indiana Standards, CCSS, and Illustrative Mathematics.

Subtraction problem where something is removed
Two parts combine into a whole (or whole decomposed into parts)
Two quantities compared to find the difference
Division where the number of groups is known, find group size

queries.description: Example queries this graph can answer. In a future iteration, these could be implemented as functions or API endpoints.
examples: Given a lesson plan, what Indiana standards does it address?
Given an Indiana standard, what Van de Walle sections and IM units cover it?
Given a topic from Van de Walle, what is the equivalent language in Indiana standards and CCSS?
What is the prerequisite chain for teaching the division algorithm?
What learning components break down a given CCSS standard into teachable pieces?
What IM units align to a given Van de Walle chapter?
